Histology · Objective 38.g

Endocrine Histology

Pituitary · Pineal · Thyroid · Adrenal · Pancreatic islets — location, hormones, blood supply & parenchyma vs stroma

Overview Parenchyma vs Stroma Pituitary Pineal Thyroid Adrenal Islets Rapid Review
1

One-Glance Comparison

Use this table to see the full objective at once before drilling each gland individually.

GlandLocationKey hormones / cellsArterial supplyVenous drainage
Pituitary Sella turcica of sphenoid, below hypothalamus Acidophils → GH, prolactin; Basophils (B-FLAT) → FSH, LH, ACTH, TSH; Neurohypophysis stores ADH & oxytocin Superior & inferior hypophyseal arteries Hypophyseal veins → cavernous sinus → IJV
Pineal Epithalamus, near roof of 3rd ventricle, between superior colliculi Pinealocytes → melatonin Posterior cerebral artery Great cerebral vein (of Galen) → straight sinus
Thyroid Anterior neck, hugging upper trachea & inferior larynx Follicular cells → T3/T4; Parafollicular C cells → calcitonin Superior thyroid a. (ext. carotid) + Inferior thyroid a. (thyrocervical trunk) ± thyroid ima a. Superior & middle thyroid vv. → IJV; Inferior thyroid vv. → brachiocephalic
Adrenal Retroperitoneal, superior pole of kidney (~T12–L1) Zona glomerulosa → aldosterone; Fasciculata → cortisol; Reticularis → androgens; Medulla → Epi/NE Superior suprarenal (inf. phrenic) + Middle suprarenal (aorta) + Inferior suprarenal (renal a.) Right → IVC direct; Left → left renal vein (± left inf. phrenic vein)
Pancreatic islets Scattered in pancreas; most numerous in tail Alpha → glucagon; Beta → insulin; Delta → somatostatin; PP cells → pancreatic polypeptide Splenic a. + superior/inferior pancreaticoduodenal arteries Splenic vein + SMV → portal vein
2

Parenchyma vs Stroma — The Core Logic

Parenchyma

The hormone-producing tissue

The cells doing the endocrine job, plus the specialized structures they form: follicles, cords, clusters, zones, or neural terminals that store or release hormone.

Stroma

The support infrastructure

Capsule, trabeculae/septa, connective tissue scaffolding, vessels, and nerves. It supports the parenchyma but does not produce hormone.

Common exam mistakes: Do NOT call thyroid colloid stroma — it is stored secretory product. Do NOT call the exocrine acini surrounding a pancreatic islet stroma — that is neighboring parenchyma of a different kind.
Exam-ready shortcut: On a histology slide, first identify the hormone-producing tissue pattern. Only then label the capsule, septa, or capillary meshwork as stroma.
3

Pituitary Gland

Mixed gland

Pituitary — Adenohypophysis + Neurohypophysis

Think: darker, cell-dense anterior lobe + paler, neural posterior lobe
Location
  • Hypophyseal fossa of the sella turcica
  • Attached to hypothalamus by the infundibulum
Adenohypophysis cells
  • Acidophils → somatotropes (GH), lactotropes (prolactin)
  • Basophils (B-FLAT) → gonadotropes (FSH, LH), corticotropes (ACTH), thyrotropes (TSH)
  • Chromophobes → poorly staining, often degranulated
Neurohypophysis
  • ADH and oxytocin produced in hypothalamus
  • Transported via axons and stored in Herring bodies
  • Released from pars nervosa
Blood supply & drainage
  • Superior hypophyseal aa. → portal system + upper stalk
  • Inferior hypophyseal aa. → posterior lobe
  • Veins → hypophyseal veins → cavernous sinus → IJV
Parenchyma vs stroma
  • Parenchyma: pars distalis, intermedia, tuberalis, and pars nervosa
  • Stroma: fine connective tissue + the portal capillary framework
4

Pineal Gland

Epithalamic gland

Pineal — Pinealocytes & Brain Sand

Think: pinealocytes + corpora arenacea (calcified concretions)
Location
  • Epithalamus, near roof of the 3rd ventricle
  • Between the superior colliculi
Cells & hormone
  • Pinealocytes → melatonin
  • Glial cells (supportive)
  • Corpora arenacea (brain sand) — calcified concretions, increase with age
Blood supply & drainage
  • Arterial supply: posterior cerebral artery
  • Venous drainage: great cerebral vein of Galen → straight sinus
Parenchyma vs stroma
  • Parenchyma: pinealocytes
  • Stroma: glial cells, CT septa, vessels
  • Corpora arenacea = identifying feature, not parenchyma
5

Thyroid Gland

Anterior neck gland

Thyroid — Follicles Full of Colloid

Think: many round follicles lined by epithelium and filled with homogeneous pink colloid
Location
  • Anterior neck, hugging upper trachea & inferior larynx
  • Right & left lobes joined by an isthmus
Cells & hormones
  • Follicular cells (thyrocytes) → T3 and T4
  • Parafollicular C cells → calcitonin
  • Colloid = stored thyroglobulin (not stroma!)
Blood supply
  • Superior thyroid a. ← external carotid
  • Inferior thyroid a. ← thyrocervical trunk
  • ± Thyroid ima a. (variable)
Venous drainage
  • Superior & middle thyroid vv. → IJV
  • Inferior thyroid vv. → brachiocephalic veins
Parenchyma vs stroma
  • Parenchyma: follicular epithelium + parafollicular C cells
  • Stroma: capsule, septa, capillary network
  • Colloid in the lumen = stored secretory product, NOT stroma
6

Adrenal Gland

Retroperitoneal gland

Adrenal — Capsule → Cortex (3 zones) → Medulla

Think: layer cake — one of the best organs for layer-based identification
Location
  • Retroperitoneal, on superior pole of each kidney
  • ~T12–L1 level
Zones & hormones
  • Zona glomerulosa → aldosterone (mineralocorticoid)
  • Zona fasciculata → cortisol (glucocorticoid)
  • Zona reticularis → androgens
  • Medulla chromaffin cells → epinephrine & norepinephrine
Arterial supply (3 sources)
  • Superior suprarenal aa. ← inferior phrenic a.
  • Middle suprarenal aa. ← abdominal aorta
  • Inferior suprarenal aa. ← renal artery
Venous drainage
  • Right suprarenal v. → IVC (direct, short)
  • Left suprarenal v. → left renal vein (± left inf. phrenic v.)
Parenchyma vs stroma
  • Parenchyma: cortical zones + medullary chromaffin tissue
  • Stroma: outer capsule, fine CT support, sinusoidal vasculature, central vein
Memory order: G-F-R-M — Glomerulosa → Fasciculata → Reticularis → Medulla (outside-in). Mnemonic: GFR = kidneys; adrenals are just above them.
7

Pancreatic Islets of Langerhans

Endocrine pancreas

Islets — Pale Endocrine Islands in a Darker Exocrine Sea

Think: pale clusters scattered throughout darker-staining exocrine acinar tissue
Location
  • Embedded within the pancreas
  • Most numerous in the tail of the pancreas
Cells & hormones
  • Alpha cells → glucagon
  • Beta cells → insulin
  • Delta cells → somatostatin
  • PP cells → pancreatic polypeptide
Blood supply
  • Splenic artery
  • Superior & inferior pancreaticoduodenal arteries
Venous drainage
  • Splenic vein + superior mesenteric vein → portal vein
Parenchyma vs stroma — key nuance
  • Endocrine parenchyma: the islet cells themselves
  • Stroma: thin reticular CT + fenestrated capillaries within/around the islet
  • Surrounding dark acini = exocrine pancreatic parenchyma, NOT stroma
Classic trap: The dark exocrine acini surrounding each islet are NOT stroma — they are a separate parenchymal compartment. Stroma is only the thin connective tissue framework.
Routine histology tip: You are expected to recognize the islet as a whole on routine H&E. Distinguishing individual alpha/beta/delta cells requires special stains or immunohistochemistry.
8

Rapid Review — Five Fast Recognition Prompts + Q&A

Pituitary

One gland, two personalities

Cell-dense anterior (darker) + paler neural posterior lobe.

Pineal

Pinealocytes + brain sand

Look for the corpora arenacea to confirm pineal tissue.

Thyroid

Round follicles with pink colloid

Lined by follicular epithelium — colloid is NOT stroma.

Adrenal

Layered gland

Capsule → glomerulosa → fasciculata → reticularis → medulla.

Pancreatic islets

Pale island in a dark sea

Light-staining clusters in darker exocrine pancreas.

1
What does the B-FLAT mnemonic cover for anterior pituitary basophils?▾
B = basophils; F = FSH (gonadotropes); L = LH (gonadotropes); A = ACTH (corticotropes); T = TSH (thyrotropes).
2
ADH and oxytocin are made where and stored where?▾
Made in the hypothalamus; stored in Herring bodies in the posterior pituitary (neurohypophysis).
3
What are corpora arenacea, and in which gland are they found?▾
Calcified concretions ('brain sand') found in the pineal gland. They increase with age and are the classic identifying feature on histology.
4
Which thyroid cells make calcitonin?▾
Parafollicular cells (C cells).
5
Is thyroid colloid stroma?▾
No — colloid is stored secretory product (thyroglobulin). Stroma is the capsule, septa, and capillary network.
6
Name the three adrenal cortical zones and their products (outside in).▾
Zona glomerulosa → aldosterone (mineralocorticoid). Zona fasciculata → cortisol (glucocorticoid). Zona reticularis → androgens.
7
How does venous drainage differ between the right and left adrenal?▾
Right suprarenal vein → IVC (short, direct). Left suprarenal vein → left renal vein (and often joins left inferior phrenic vein first).
8
Which islet cells make insulin?▾
Beta cells.
9
Is the exocrine pancreas surrounding a pancreatic islet considered stroma?▾
No — it is a separate parenchymal compartment (exocrine). Stroma is only the thin reticular connective tissue and capillaries within and around the islet.
10
Where does venous blood from the pancreatic islets ultimately drain?▾
Splenic vein and superior mesenteric vein → portal vein → liver.
9

Exam-Style MCQs

These are built to force histology recognition first, then function.

1
A histology slide shows endocrine cells arranged in cords with scattered calcified concretions known as brain sand. Which gland is represented?
A. Posterior pituitary
B. Pineal gland
C. Thyroid gland
D. Adrenal medulla
▾
Answer: B. Pineal gland. Corpora arenacea, or brain sand, are the classic identifying feature of the pineal gland. Posterior pituitary shows pituicytes and Herring bodies, thyroid shows colloid-filled follicles, and adrenal medulla contains chromaffin cells without calcified concretions.
2
A section of adrenal gland shows a broad middle cortical zone composed of pale, lipid-rich cells arranged in long straight cords. Which product is most closely associated with this zone?
A. Aldosterone
B. Cortisol
C. Epinephrine
D. Calcitonin
▾
Answer: B. Cortisol. The broad middle zone is the zona fasciculata, which produces glucocorticoids, especially cortisol. Aldosterone comes from zona glomerulosa, epinephrine from the adrenal medulla, and calcitonin from thyroid C cells.
✕
